source: Hanson & Sigman (2021), replication archive (Harvard Dataverse DOI 10.7910/DVN/IFZXQX, CC0)
citation: Hanson, Jonathan K. and Rachel Sigman. 2021. 'Leviathan's Latent Dimensions: Measuring State Capacity for Comparative Political Research.' Journal of Politics 83(4): 1495–1510.

extractive.label: Extractive capacity
extractive.description: The state's ability to extract resources — chiefly tax revenue — from society. Motivated by Tilly (1990), Levi (1988), and Besley & Persson (2009). H&S follow Lieberman (2002) in distinguishing administratively complex tax bases from easy-to-collect ones.

indicators.label: Total tax revenue (% of GDP)
indicators.description: Overall extractive effort. Excludes nontax revenues (resource rents, trade taxes don't require the enforcement and information apparatus that taxation of domestic activity does).

indicators.label: Income taxes (% of total taxes)
indicators.description: Reliance on administratively complex tax bases — income, property, consumption — that require record-keeping and a sophisticated bureaucracy. Higher share signals stronger administrative reach as well as extractive depth.

indicators.label: Trade taxes (% of total taxes)
indicators.description: Inverse signal — trade taxes are cheap to collect at the border. Heavy reliance on them indicates the state lacks the apparatus to tax domestic activity. Enters the H&S model with a negative loading.

indicators.label: Efficiency of revenue mobilization (CPIA)
indicators.description: Expert-coded rating of how efficiently the state mobilizes revenue. IDA-eligible countries only, 2005-2015.

indicators.label: Fiscal capacity (V-Dem)
indicators.description: Expert assessment of the extent to which the state can fund itself through taxes of greater administrative complexity. A direct V-Dem analogue of the Lieberman logic.

coercive.label: Coercive capacity
coercive.description: The state's monopoly on legitimate violence and the institutionalization of the means of coercion. H&S pair quantitative personnel/spending measures with expert-coded indicators of order and 'stateness', warning that raw force size can signal either capacity or insecurity.

indicators.label: Military expenditures per capita (log)
indicators.description: Logged USD per capita. Resources devoted to military capability — logging dampens influence of oil-state and war-economy outliers.

indicators.label: Military personnel per 1,000 (log)
indicators.description: Logged size of armed forces relative to population. Long-standing Correlates-of-War measure of coercive reach.

indicators.label: Police officers per 1,000 (log)
indicators.source: UN Office on Drugs and Crime
indicators.description: Logged size of the internal security apparatus. Distinguishes domestic order maintenance from external defense.

indicators.label: Monopoly on use of force (BTI)
indicators.description: BTI expert rating of the degree to which the state actually holds a monopoly on legitimate violence across its territory. Note: the H&S replication column ranges 0–18 (a non-standard rescaling); for the canonical BTI 1–10 series see the BTI Monopoly on Use of Force entry under Explore related indicators.

indicators.label: State authority over territory
indicators.description: Percentage of territory controlled by the central state, converted via the inverse cumulative normal. Captures 'stateness' in the territorial sense.

indicators.label: State antiquity index
indicators.source: Bockstette, Chanda & Putterman (2002), extended by H&S
indicators.description: Historical depth of state institutions on a given territory (50-year discounting). Components: presence of a state, share of territory controlled, sovereignty. Long-run capacity to hold territory as the deepest indicator of institutionalized coercion.

administrative.label: Administrative capacity
administrative.description: The state's ability to formulate policy, deliver services, keep records, and execute decisions through a professional bureaucracy. H&S pull in component-level expert ratings plus a distinct cluster of information-gathering indicators that capture the state's ability to see its own population.

indicators.label: Rigorous and impartial public administration
indicators.description: Expert survey ratings of whether public officials respect the law rigorously and impartially — the rule-of-law-in-administration dimension of Weberian bureaucracy.

indicators.label: Administrative efficiency (Adelman & Morris)
indicators.description: Early cross-national rating of administrative apparatus efficiency. Covers only 1960-62 but is one of very few series reaching that far back.

indicators.label: Weberianness (Rauch & Evans)
indicators.description: Index of meritocratic recruitment and career stability in the core economic bureaucracy. The operational benchmark for 'Weberian' bureaucracies. 34 countries, 1970-90.

indicators.label: Quality of budgetary & financial management (CPIA)
indicators.description: Expert rating of technical competence of fiscal management. IDA-eligible countries only, 2005-2015.

indicators.label: Quality of public administration (CPIA)
indicators.description: Expert rating of broader public-administration capability — policy coordination, civil service, service delivery. IDA-eligible countries only.

indicators.label: Census frequency
indicators.description: Annualised frequency of national censuses, computed by looking forward and backward from each year to the nearest census records. Higher values = more frequent censuses = stronger information capacity. Range in the data ≈ 0.1–2.4.

indicators.label: Information capacity (Brambor et al.)
indicators.description: Composite of whether the state operates a statistical agency, civil register, and population register, plus its capabilities for producing a census and a statistical yearbook.
